_meta.role: Onboarding & Enablement Program Manager, FDE (Forward Deployed Engineering)
_meta.what_firm_does: OpenAI is the AI research and deployment company behind ChatGPT and the GPT models; its mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity. Forward Deployed Engineers embed with customers to ship AI solutions in the field.
_meta.jd_wants: A program manager to build onboarding and enablement for a fast-growing technical (FDE) team — reusable playbooks, ramp pathways, AI-native workflows, and the measurement to prove they shorten time-to-impact.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role?: Enablement for a fast-moving technical team isn't just about onboarding decks—from my experience, it's about building systems that compound: reusable playbooks and workflows that let a small team have outsized impact instead of growing headcount linearly. That's the work I'm drawn to. At InGenius Prep I build the enablement infrastructure—onboarding pathways, playbooks, knowledge assets—that ramps new people, and I deliberately codify each cycle's lessons into reusable resources rather than relying on one-off heroics, which cut ramp time by 75%. Doing that for OpenAI's Forward Deployed Engineers, where the work is technical and the team is scaling fast, is a problem I'd genuinely enjoy: make the system stronger every cycle so people get to impact faster.
Why OpenAI?: Honestly, two things. The mission—ensuring AI benefits all of humanity—is work I'd be proud to enable from behind the scenes. And the FDE model itself is the draw: enabling engineers who embed with customers means the enablement has to be practical, fast, and AI-native, not theoretical. I already use AI tools daily in my own program work to automate the repetitive parts, so building an AI-first enablement function feels like an extension of how I already operate rather than a leap. I'd rather build the ramp system for a team doing consequential field work than ship generic training somewhere the stakes are lower.
What excites you about this opportunity?: What excites me is that this is enablement as a leverage problem, not a content problem. A Forward Deployed team can't scale by hiring linearly—it scales when knowledge gets captured, reused, and improved. At NextGen Healthcare I analyzed 2,000+ employee experiences and turned scattered feedback into structured guidance that drove more consistent execution, which is the analytical backbone of how I'd measure ramp speed and time-to-first-deployment here. The chance to design the system that decides how fast a new FDE becomes effective—and to treat adoption and feedback signals as the real scoreboard rather than 'training delivered'—is exactly the kind of compounding work I want to own.
Tell us about a time you built enablemen: At Vestas Wind Systems, I piloted an Inclusive Leadership workshop for senior leaders that later scaled to 23 global locations, and I optimized onboarding across three international sites. The challenge wasn't designing one good session—it was building something that landed consistently across very different teams and contexts while still flexing to local needs. I did it by defining the shared standards that had to hold everywhere, then leaving room for role-specific adaptation, and tracking how it landed as it expanded. That's proof I can design enablement that balances common playbooks with the role-specific reality of an FDE team. It also taught me that scalable enablement is a system you tune, not a deliverable you ship once.
